top_regulatory_deltas.name: Primary school and early childhood teachers

children.name: Commissioned armed forces officers
children.rationale: Military officers blend digital command/control systems with physical operations, strategic planning, and real-time human leadership in unpredictable environments. While AI can enhance intelligence analysis, logistics optimization, and some planning functions, core military leadership requires human judgment in life-or-death situations with high uncertainty.
children.regulatory_friction: Military operations fall under specialized defense regulations beyond civilian AI Act provisions, but any AI systems affecting personnel decisions (promotions, assignments) would trigger works council consultation requirements. National security considerations create additional barriers to AI adoption in sensitive command functions.
key_vulnerable_tasks: analyse potential threats against national security
children.uk_rationale: Military command and strategic decision-making face minimal UK civilian regulatory friction due to Crown exemptions and military-specific governance frameworks. Defence AI deployment operates under MOD protocols rather than civilian data protection or employment law constraints.
eu_obligations: AI Act Art 26(7): Employer must inform worker representatives before deploying high-risk AI
AI Act Art 14: Human oversight required for AI-assisted employment decisions
GDPR Art 22: Right against solely automated decisions with legal/significant effects

works_council_de.description: Co-determination required for introduction and use of technical devices designed to monitor employee behaviour or performance. Works council must agree before any AI monitoring system is deployed. §87(1) Nr. 7 additionally covers health and safety aspects of AI workplace tools.

works_council_at.description: Works council consent required for introduction of systems that automate collection, processing and transmission of personal employee data (§96a(1) Nr. 1). Separate consent required for systems assessing employee behaviour (§96(1) Nr. 3). §91 provides additional information and consultation rights.

switzerland.description: FADP Art 21: Duty to inform data subjects about automated individual decisions with legal consequence or considerable adverse effect. OR Art 328b: Employer data processing limited to job-suitability and contract-performance purposes (stricter scope than GDPR, civil law enforcement). ArGV3 Art 26: Monitoring systems whose sole/main purpose is monitoring employee behaviour are prohibited; permitted only for legitimate purposes (safety, work organisation) if proportionate and employees informed. Mitwirkungsgesetz (SR 822.14): Information and consultation rights only — no co-determination. Employers must inform and consult but NOT obtain consent.

children.subject_explanation: Workers in 'Commissioned armed forces officers' are subject to AI-assisted recruitment screening, performance evaluation, task allocation, and workforce management under Annex III category 4 (employment). Employers deploying AI systems for these purposes must comply with high-risk requirements.
children.deployer_explanation: 'Commissioned armed forces officers' does not inherently involve deploying high-risk AI systems on others, though individual employers in this sector may use such systems.

children.name: Non-commissioned armed forces officers
children.rationale: Non-commissioned officers operate in highly dynamic, unstructured environments requiring real-time tactical decisions, physical presence, and human leadership under extreme conditions. While AI can assist with intelligence analysis and threat identification, core duties like squad leadership, combat operations, and split-second tactical decisions in chaotic environments remain largely beyond current AI capabilities.
children.regulatory_friction: Military operations fall under national security exemptions from most EU regulations including the AI Act and GDPR. Additionally, the life-or-death nature of combat operations and strict military command structures create institutional barriers to AI adoption in core operational roles.
children.uk_rationale: UK armed forces operate under military law and defence-specific regulations that heavily restrict AI deployment in tactical decision-making and personnel management. While the UK lacks EU-style AI regulation, military command structures and national security protocols create substantial barriers to AI automation in combat and leadership roles.

GDPR Art 22: Right against solely automated decisions with legal/significant effects

children.deployer_explanation: 'Non-commissioned armed forces officers' does not inherently involve deploying high-risk AI systems on others, though individual employers in this sector may use such systems.

children.name: Armed forces occupations, other ranks
children.rationale: Armed forces other ranks involve primarily physical, high-stakes operations requiring real-time decision-making in unstructured environments with significant safety implications. While some roles like intelligence communications have digital components that AI could assist with, the core functions demand physical presence, manual dexterity, and human judgment in life-or-death situations.
children.regulatory_friction: Military operations are subject to extremely strict command structures, national security protocols, and international laws of armed conflict that severely limit AI adoption. The life-or-death nature of military decisions requires human accountability that regulatory frameworks cannot delegate to AI systems.
children.uk_rationale: Military personnel operate under military law and chain of command rather than civilian employment regulations, with Equality Act and UK GDPR having limited application in operational contexts. Defence exemptions and national security considerations further reduce civilian regulatory friction for AI deployment in military operations.

GDPR Art 22: Right against solely automated decisions with legal/significant effects

children.name: Legislators and senior officials
children.rationale: Legislators and senior officials have moderate AI exposure primarily through research, policy analysis, and administrative support tasks. AI can assist with analyzing legislation, economic trends, drafting documents, and managing information flows, but core functions require political judgment, stakeholder relationship management, and public representation that remain fundamentally human.
children.regulatory_friction: EU AI Act Annex III creates massive barriers as AI systems for public administration and governance would be classified as high-risk, requiring extensive oversight. Democratic accountability principles, constitutional requirements for human decision-making in governance, and strong employment protections for public officials create nearly insurmountable regulatory barriers to AI displacement.
children.uk_rationale: UK's lighter regulatory approach reduces friction for AI adoption in high-level governmental functions compared to EU constraints. However, constitutional conventions around democratic accountability and potential judicial review of automated decision-making in public administration create meaningful limitations.

children.deployer_explanation: 'Legislators and senior officials' may deploy AI for recruitment, evaluation, task allocation, or performance monitoring (Annex III cat. 4); AI for legal research, applying law, or dispute resolution (Annex III cat. 8).

children.name: Managing directors and chief executives
children.rationale: Managing directors and chief executives work with highly digital information flows - financial data, strategic reports, market analysis, and performance metrics that AI can process and synthesize effectively. AI can automate much of the data analysis, reporting, and even strategic recommendation generation that forms the foundation of executive decision-making.
children.regulatory_friction: EU AI Act Annex III creates significant barriers as executive decisions often involve high-risk AI applications in workforce management and performance evaluation. Works council co-determination rights require consultation for any AI systems affecting working conditions, substantially slowing adoption of AI-powered management tools.
children.uk_rationale: UK's pro-innovation approach creates lower friction for C-suite AI adoption compared to EU, with minimal regulatory barriers for strategic decision-making tools. However, Equality Act discrimination risks in workforce decisions and UK GDPR automated decision-making provisions still create some compliance considerations for executive-level AI deployment.

children.name: Business services and administration managers
children.rationale: Business services and administration managers perform largely digital work including strategic planning, financial analysis, compliance monitoring, and process optimization that AI can significantly enhance or automate. Many core tasks involve data analysis, report generation, policy development, and administrative coordination that are highly susceptible to AI transformation.
children.regulatory_friction: EU AI Act Annex III creates high-risk classifications for HR managers and talent acquisition managers in recruitment and performance evaluation. Works council co-determination rights under BetrVG §87 and ArbVG §96a require consultation for any AI affecting working conditions, significantly slowing adoption across all management functions.
children.uk_rationale: UK's light-touch regulation allows broader AI deployment in business management functions compared to EU. Equality Act 2010 creates some friction for AI-assisted HR decisions and talent management, while UK GDPR Article 22 applies to automated decision-making in procurement and employee management.

children.name: Sales, marketing and development managers
children.rationale: Sales, marketing and development managers work extensively with digital tools, data analysis, content creation, and strategic planning - all areas where AI excels. AI can automate market research, consumer trend analysis, campaign optimization, content generation, and predictive analytics for product development.
children.regulatory_friction: EU AI Act Annex III creates barriers for AI systems used in recruitment and performance evaluation of teams. Works council co-determination rights require consultation for AI tools affecting working conditions, and GDPR limits automated decision-making in customer profiling and targeting.
children.uk_rationale: UK's pro-innovation stance significantly reduces friction compared to EU, particularly for strategic decision-making roles. However, Equality Act creates meaningful liability risk for AI-assisted hiring/promotion decisions, and UK GDPR Art 22 applies to automated customer profiling and targeting.

children.name: Production managers in agriculture, forestry and fisheries
children.rationale: Production managers in agriculture, forestry and fisheries work in a mixed digital-physical environment where AI can significantly enhance planning, monitoring, and optimization tasks through data analysis and predictive modeling. However, the role requires substantial field presence, hands-on management of living systems, and real-time decision-making in unstructured outdoor environments that limit full automation potential.
children.regulatory_friction: Works council co-determination rights under BetrVG §87 and ArbVG §96a create consultation requirements for AI systems affecting workforce management and working conditions. GDPR constraints on automated decision-making also slow adoption of AI tools for performance evaluation and resource allocation decisions.

children.name: Manufacturing, mining, construction, and distribution managers
children.rationale: These managers perform largely digital tasks including planning, scheduling, forecasting, supply chain optimization, and data analysis that AI can significantly enhance or automate. However, they require substantial human judgment for strategic decisions, stakeholder management, and handling complex operational exceptions in physical environments.
children.regulatory_friction: Works council co-determination rights under BetrVG §87 require consultation for AI systems affecting workforce management and performance evaluation. GDPR restrictions on automated decision-making limit AI deployment in personnel and operational decisions.
children.uk_rationale: Manufacturing and distribution managers face moderate UK regulatory friction from Equality Act 2010 claims if AI systems create discriminatory hiring/promotion outcomes, and UK GDPR Article 22 requirements for automated decision-making transparency. However, the UK's pro-innovation stance and absence of EU AI Act equivalents significantly reduce barriers compared to EU regulation.

AI can automate documentation processes, analyze system performance metrics, generate project reports, and support strategic decision-making through data insights. However, senior leadership roles require complex stakeholder management, strategic vision, and accountability that still need human oversight.
children.regulatory_friction: EU AI Act Annex III classifications for workforce management systems and performance evaluation create significant barriers, as these managers often implement AI systems affecting employees. children.name: Professional services managers

children.regulatory_friction: EU AI Act Annex III creates major barriers as many managers oversee recruitment, performance evaluation, and workforce management systems. Works council co-determination rights require consultation for any AI affecting working conditions, while employment protection laws slow workforce restructuring in management roles.

Sector-specific regulators like CQC (healthcare), Ofsted (education), and FCA (financial services) may impose additional AI governance requirements, but the UK's pro-innovation approach allows significant deployment flexibility compared to EU constraints.

switzerland.description: FADP Art 21: Duty to inform data subjects about automated individual decisions with legal consequence or considerable adverse effect. children.name: Retail and wholesale trade managers
children.rationale: Retail and wholesale trade managers perform many digitally-native tasks including inventory management, sales analytics, budgeting, scheduling, and supplier coordination that AI can significantly enhance or automate. AI can optimize pricing, predict demand, automate ordering, and provide customer insights, while digital tools increasingly handle administrative workflows.
children.regulatory_friction: EU AI Act Annex III classifications around workforce management systems and German/Austrian works council requirements for AI affecting employee scheduling, performance monitoring, or working conditions create significant barriers. GDPR also restricts automated customer profiling and decision-making systems.

Primary friction comes from Equality Act discrimination risks in AI-assisted hiring/promotion decisions and UK GDPR Article 22 constraints on automated customer profiling, but absence of EU AI Act equivalent significantly reduces compliance burden.

children.regulatory_friction: Works council co-determination under BetrVG §87 requires consultation when AI systems affect working conditions or performance evaluation. GDPR Article 22 restricts automated decision-making in engineering assessments that affect individuals or projects.

children.name: Veterinarians
children.rationale: Veterinary work combines high technical knowledge with significant hands-on physical examination, surgery, and direct animal interaction. AI can assist with diagnostic imaging analysis, treatment recommendations, and administrative tasks, but the core work requires physical presence, manual dexterity, and real-time assessment of living animals in unstructured environments.
children.regulatory_friction: GDPR creates constraints around automated health decisions, while strong employment protections in veterinary practices slow workforce restructuring. The EU AI Act's high-risk classifications don't directly apply, but medical device regulations govern AI diagnostic tools.
children.uk_rationale: Veterinary practice faces moderate regulatory friction through professional standards requirements from the Royal College of Veterinary Surgeons (RCVS) and potential UK GDPR obligations for client data processing. The Equality Act 2010 creates limited friction as veterinary decisions primarily concern animal welfare rather than human discrimination, though client-facing AI tools could raise concerns.
eu_obligations: AI Act Art 26(7): Employer must inform worker representatives before deploying high-risk AI
AI Act Art 14: Human oversight required for AI-assisted employment decisions
GDPR Art 22: Right against solely automated decisions with legal/significant effects

works_council_de.description: Co-determination required for introduction and use of technical devices designed to monitor employee behaviour or performance. Works council must agree before any AI monitoring system is deployed. §87(1) Nr. 7 additionally covers health and safety aspects of AI workplace tools.

works_council_at.description: Works council consent required for introduction of systems that automate collection, processing and transmission of personal employee data (§96a(1) Nr. 1). Separate consent required for systems assessing employee behaviour (§96(1) Nr. 3). §91 provides additional information and consultation rights.

switzerland.description: FADP Art 21: Duty to inform data subjects about automated individual decisions with legal consequence or considerable adverse effect. OR Art 328b: Employer data processing limited to job-suitability and contract-performance purposes (stricter scope than GDPR, civil law enforcement). ArGV3 Art 26: Monitoring systems whose sole/main purpose is monitoring employee behaviour are prohibited; permitted only for legitimate purposes (safety, work organisation) if proportionate and employees informed. Mitwirkungsgesetz (SR 822.14): Information and consultation rights only — no co-determination. Employers must inform and consult but NOT obtain consent.

children.subject_explanation: Workers in 'Veterinarians' are subject to AI-assisted recruitment screening, performance evaluation, task allocation, and workforce management under Annex III category 4 (employment). Employers deploying AI systems for these purposes must comply with high-risk requirements.
children.deployer_explanation: 'Veterinarians' does not inherently involve deploying high-risk AI systems on others, though individual employers in this sector may use such systems.

children.name: Other health professionals
children.rationale: Other health professionals work with mixed digital-physical tasks, where AI can significantly assist in diagnostics, image analysis, treatment planning, and clinical decision support. However, these roles require specialized clinical judgment, direct patient interaction, and manual procedures that create natural barriers to full automation.
children.regulatory_friction: Healthcare AI systems face strict EU AI Act high-risk classifications for medical devices and health applications, requiring extensive validation and approval processes. GDPR Article 22 restricts automated decision-making in healthcare, while strong employment protections in healthcare sectors slow workforce restructuring.
key_protected_tasks: complex clinical decision-making requiring human judgment
children.uk_rationale: UK's sector-specific regulation creates meaningful friction - CQC oversight of diagnostic AI, MHRA device regulation, and professional body standards limit deployment speed. However, absence of EU AI Act's high-risk healthcare classifications and mandatory worker consultation requirements reduces friction compared to EU.

children.name: University and higher education teachers
children.rationale: University teachers work heavily with digital content creation, research, grading, and knowledge synthesis - all areas where AI excels. AI can assist with lecture preparation, automated grading, research analysis, and content generation, though the role requires complex human judgment in pedagogy and original research.
children.regulatory_friction: EU AI Act Annex III classifies AI systems in education as high-risk, requiring strict compliance and human oversight. GDPR Article 22 restricts automated decision-making in student evaluation, while works council co-determination rights require consultation for any AI affecting academic working conditions.
children.uk_rationale: UK universities face moderate friction from Equality Act 2010 claims regarding AI-assisted student assessment and admissions, plus UK GDPR Article 22 requirements for automated decision-making in grading systems. However, the absence of EU AI Act equivalents and weak consultation requirements create significantly less friction than continental Europe.

children.name: Vocational education teachers
children.rationale: Vocational education teachers have high AI exposure because much of their work involves creating curricula, lesson plans, assessments, and educational materials - fundamentally digital work products that AI can generate effectively. AI can also provide personalized learning experiences, simulate training scenarios, and deliver standardized theoretical content across many vocational domains.
children.regulatory_friction: The EU AI Act classifies AI systems in education as high-risk (Annex III), requiring strict compliance measures. Works council co-determination rights mean any AI affecting teaching methods or performance evaluation requires consultation, while strong employment protections in education slow workforce restructuring.
children.uk_rationale: Limited regulatory friction in UK education sector compared to EU. Equality Act 2010 provides some protection against discriminatory AI use in student assessment, but no sector-specific AI restrictions or mandatory consultation requirements for vocational training institutions.

children.regulatory_friction: EU AI Act Annex III classifies AI systems in education as high-risk, requiring conformity assessments and human oversight. Works council co-determination rights require consultation on any AI affecting teaching conditions or student evaluation methods.

However, Equality Act concerns around AI-assisted grading and student evaluation, plus UK GDPR requirements for automated decision-making affecting student outcomes, create moderate friction.

children.name: Primary school and early childhood teachers
children.rationale: Primary and early childhood teachers have moderate AI exposure as their work involves substantial digital content creation (lesson plans, assessments, educational materials) and administrative tasks that AI can enhance or automate. children.regulatory_friction: The EU AI Act classifies AI systems in education as high-risk (Annex III), requiring strict compliance and human oversight. Works council consultation rights under German BetrVG §87 and Austrian ArbVG for any AI affecting working conditions, combined with strong teacher employment protections, create substantial barriers to AI-driven workforce restructuring.
children.uk_rationale: UK education sector has moderate regulatory friction through Equality Act requirements for fair assessment and UK GDPR protections for children's data, but lacks EU-style AI Act restrictions on high-risk educational applications. The Department for Education's guidance on AI in schools is non-binding, creating lower barriers than EU frameworks while maintaining child protection standards.

children.name: Finance professionals
children.rationale: Finance professionals work primarily with digital data, financial models, and analytical tasks that AI excels at - pattern recognition in markets, automated reporting, risk calculations, and document analysis. Many routine tasks like basic auditing, financial statement preparation, and standard investment analysis are highly susceptible to AI automation.
children.regulatory_friction: EU AI Act Annex III covers AI systems for creditworthiness assessment and loan decisions, while GDPR Article 22 restricts automated financial decision-making. Strong employment protections and works council rights in countries like Germany slow workforce restructuring in the heavily regulated financial sector.
children.uk_rationale: Finance professionals face moderate regulatory friction in the UK through FCA oversight requiring explainability for algorithmic decisions affecting consumers, UK GDPR Article 22 restrictions on automated financial decision-making, and Equality Act risks in AI-assisted hiring and performance evaluation. However, the UK's pro-innovation stance and absence of EU AI Act requirements significantly reduces friction compared to EU counterparts.

children.name: Administration professionals
children.rationale: Administration professionals work extensively with digital information processing, data analysis, policy research, and report writing - all areas where AI excels. children.regulatory_friction: EU AI Act Annex III creates significant barriers for HR officers and recruitment consultants performing workforce management. children.uk_rationale: UK's pro-innovation approach creates less friction than EU for administration professionals using AI tools for analysis, reporting, and decision support. However, HR-related roles face Equality Act 2010 discrimination risks in recruitment/promotion, and policy officers must consider UK GDPR Article 22 for automated public administration decisions.

children.name: Sales, marketing and public relations professionals
children.rationale: This occupation group has high AI exposure as most work is digitally-driven, involving content creation, data analysis, customer communication, and strategic planning that AI can increasingly automate or augment. children.regulatory_friction: EU AI Act Annex III creates barriers for AI systems used in recruitment and workforce management (affecting HR-related marketing roles). key_protected_tasks: complex B2B technical sales requiring deep expertise
regulatory and policy advocacy requiring human credibility

children.name: Database and network professionals
children.rationale: Database and network professionals work entirely in digital environments with highly structured, rule-based tasks that AI can increasingly automate - from network configuration and monitoring to database optimization and security threat detection. AI code generation, automated system administration, and intelligent network management tools are rapidly advancing to handle routine maintenance, troubleshooting, and even architectural decisions.
children.regulatory_friction: GDPR Article 22 restrictions on automated decision-making limit AI deployment in access control and user management systems. Works council co-determination rights under BetrVG §87 require consultation when AI tools change working conditions or monitoring practices in IT departments.

children.name: Legal professionals
children.rationale: Legal professionals work heavily with digital documents, legal research, contract analysis, and regulatory compliance - all areas where AI excels. children.uk_rationale: UK's light-touch AI regulation significantly reduces friction compared to EU, but legal profession faces meaningful constraints from UK GDPR Article 22 on automated decision-making and Equality Act 2010 discrimination risks in hiring/case assessment. children.regulatory_friction: Critical infrastructure sectors (energy, water, waste) face stringent safety regulations that slow AI adoption. children.uk_rationale: Process control in critical infrastructure (power, water, chemical plants) faces sector-specific regulatory oversight from bodies like Ofgem and Environment Agency, creating moderate friction for AI deployment. children.name: Waiters and bartenders
children.rationale: Waiters and bartenders work in highly physical, real-time customer service environments requiring manual dexterity, immediate human interaction, and navigation of unstructured physical spaces. children.regulatory_friction: GDPR constraints on automated customer profiling and recommendation systems create some friction. Works council consultation requirements for any AI systems affecting working conditions (like automated scheduling or performance monitoring) add regulatory barriers.

children.uk_rationale: The UK's pro-innovation regulatory approach creates minimal friction for AI deployment in hospitality service roles. UK GDPR Art 22 has limited relevance as AI applications focus on operational optimization rather than automated decision-making about individuals.

children.rationale: Wood processing and papermaking plant operators primarily perform hands-on industrial tasks requiring real-time equipment monitoring, physical material handling, and safety-critical decision-making in manufacturing environments. While some process optimization and quality control aspects could benefit from AI assistance, the core work involves operating complex machinery with physical materials that require human oversight and manual intervention.
children.regulatory_friction: Works council co-determination rights under BetrVG §87 require consultation for AI systems affecting working conditions, potentially slowing adoption of automated monitoring or optimization systems. General employment protection laws may also slow workforce restructuring even where AI enables productivity gains.
children.uk_rationale: Wood processing and papermaking plant operators face minimal AI deployment, making regulation largely irrelevant. The UK's light-touch regulatory approach provides slightly more flexibility than EU frameworks for any automated process optimization or safety monitoring systems that might be introduced.
eu_obligations: AI Act Art 26(7): Employer must inform worker representatives before deploying high-risk AI
AI Act Art 14: Human oversight required for AI-assisted employment decisions
GDPR Art 22: Right against solely automated decisions with legal/significant effects

works_council_de.description: Co-determination required for introduction and use of technical devices designed to monitor employee behaviour or performance. Works council must agree before any AI monitoring system is deployed. §87(1) Nr. 7 additionally covers health and safety aspects of AI workplace tools.

works_council_at.description: Works council consent required for introduction of systems that automate collection, processing and transmission of personal employee data (§96a(1) Nr. 1). Separate consent required for systems assessing employee behaviour (§96(1) Nr. 3). §91 provides additional information and consultation rights.

switzerland.description: FADP Art 21: Duty to inform data subjects about automated individual decisions with legal consequence or considerable adverse effect. OR Art 328b: Employer data processing limited to job-suitability and contract-performance purposes (stricter scope than GDPR, civil law enforcement). ArGV3 Art 26: Monitoring systems whose sole/main purpose is monitoring employee behaviour are prohibited; permitted only for legitimate purposes (safety, work organisation) if proportionate and employees informed. Mitwirkungsgesetz (SR 822.14): Information and consultation rights only — no co-determination. Employers must inform and consult but NOT obtain consent.

children.subject_explanation: Workers in 'Wood processing and papermaking plant operators' are subject to AI-assisted recruitment screening, performance evaluation, task allocation, and workforce management under Annex III category 4 (employment). Employers deploying AI systems for these purposes must comply with high-risk requirements.
children.deployer_explanation: 'Wood processing and papermaking plant operators' does not inherently involve deploying high-risk AI systems on others, though individual employers in this sector may use such systems.

children.name: Manufacturing labourers
children.rationale: Manufacturing labourers work primarily in physical environments requiring manual dexterity, hand-eye coordination, and real-time adaptation to varied physical materials and conditions. While some tasks like inventory tracking or quality checking could benefit from AI assistance, the core work involves tactile skills and physical manipulation that current AI cannot replicate.
children.regulatory_friction: Works council co-determination rights under BetrVG §87(1) No. 6 require consultation for any AI systems affecting working conditions, slowing adoption of AI-powered monitoring or task allocation systems in manufacturing environments.
children.uk_rationale: Manufacturing labourers face minimal AI exposure in their primarily physical, manual tasks, making regulatory friction nearly irrelevant. The UK's light-touch approach creates marginally less friction than EU regulation for the limited AI applications that might affect these roles (basic scheduling, task allocation systems).
eu_obligations: AI Act Art 26(7): Employer must inform worker representatives before deploying high-risk AI
AI Act Art 14: Human oversight required for AI-assisted employment decisions
GDPR Art 22: Right against solely automated decisions with legal/significant effects

works_council_de.description: Co-determination required for introduction and use of technical devices designed to monitor employee behaviour or performance. Works council must agree before any AI monitoring system is deployed. §87(1) Nr. 7 additionally covers health and safety aspects of AI workplace tools.

works_council_at.description: Works council consent required for introduction of systems that automate collection, processing and transmission of personal employee data (§96a(1) Nr. 1). Separate consent required for systems assessing employee behaviour (§96(1) Nr. 3). §91 provides additional information and consultation rights.

switzerland.description: FADP Art 21: Duty to inform data subjects about automated individual decisions with legal consequence or considerable adverse effect. OR Art 328b: Employer data processing limited to job-suitability and contract-performance purposes (stricter scope than GDPR, civil law enforcement). ArGV3 Art 26: Monitoring systems whose sole/main purpose is monitoring employee behaviour are prohibited; permitted only for legitimate purposes (safety, work organisation) if proportionate and employees informed. Mitwirkungsgesetz (SR 822.14): Information and consultation rights only — no co-determination. Employers must inform and consult but NOT obtain consent.

children.subject_explanation: Workers in 'Manufacturing labourers' are subject to AI-assisted recruitment screening, performance evaluation, task allocation, and workforce management under Annex III category 4 (employment). Employers deploying AI systems for these purposes must comply with high-risk requirements.
children.deployer_explanation: 'Manufacturing labourers' does not inherently involve deploying high-risk AI systems on others, though individual employers in this sector may use such systems.

children.name: Transport and storage labourers
children.rationale: Transport and storage laborers work primarily in physical environments requiring manual handling, spatial reasoning, and real-time problem-solving in unstructured settings. While some logistics coordination tasks could be AI-assisted, the core work involves physical manipulation of goods, human judgment for proper handling, and adaptation to varying physical conditions that current AI cannot directly perform.
children.regulatory_friction: Works council co-determination rights under BetrVG §87 and ArbVG §96a require consultation for AI systems affecting working conditions, particularly warehouse management systems. GDPR restrictions on automated decision-making may limit AI-driven task assignment and performance monitoring systems.
key_protected_tasks: manual handling and lifting of goods
real-time problem solving for damaged or irregular items
children.uk_rationale: Transport and storage labourers have minimal regulatory friction in the UK as AI deployment is limited to basic warehouse management systems and route optimization. The Equality Act 2010 provides minimal constraint on algorithmic scheduling or task assignment systems, while working time regulations may limit intensive AI-driven productivity monitoring.

AI Act Art 14: Human oversight required for AI-assisted employment decisions
GDPR Art 22: Right against solely automated decisions with legal/significant effects

children.name: Food preparation assistants
children.rationale: Food preparation assistants work primarily in physical environments requiring manual tasks like washing dishes, cleaning surfaces, basic food prep, and stock handling. While some ordering/inventory systems could be automated, the core work involves physical manipulation in unstructured kitchen environments with real-time coordination.
children.regulatory_friction: GDPR constraints on automated workforce scheduling and the EU AI Act's restrictions on AI systems for performance evaluation create modest friction. Works council rights in countries like Germany require consultation for any AI affecting working conditions.
children.uk_rationale: Food preparation assistants have minimal AI exposure limited to basic scheduling or inventory systems. UK's lighter regulatory approach removes EU friction around worker consultation for these simple applications. UK GDPR automated decision-making provisions have minimal relevance for basic operational tools.

children.name: Street and related service workers
children.rationale: Leaflet distribution requires physical presence in specific geographic locations, manual dexterity for handling materials, and real-time navigation of outdoor environments and human interactions. While route optimization could be AI-assisted, the core work is fundamentally physical and location-dependent.
children.regulatory_friction: Limited regulatory friction as this occupation rarely involves AI systems that would trigger EU AI Act high-risk classifications or GDPR automated decision-making concerns. Basic employment protections may slow any workforce restructuring.
children.uk_rationale: Street service workers like leaflet distributors have minimal AI exposure and face negligible UK regulatory friction. The UK's lighter regulatory approach creates slightly less friction than EU frameworks, but this is barely relevant given the physical, low-skill nature of the work.

children.name: Street vendors (excluding food)
children.rationale: Street vendors work in unstructured physical environments requiring real-time human interaction, physical mobility, and manual handling of goods. While AI could assist with inventory management and payment processing, the core work of face-to-face selling, product demonstration, and navigating street locations requires human presence and adaptability.
children.regulatory_friction: GDPR creates minor friction around customer data collection and automated payment systems. Employment protection laws may slow adoption of AI-assisted vendor management systems in organized retail contexts.
children.uk_rationale: Street vendors have minimal AI exposure given the physical, interpersonal nature of street selling. The UK's lighter regulatory framework offers virtually no friction for the limited AI applications that might exist (basic payment processing, simple inventory apps).

children.regulatory_friction: GDPR may apply to any AI systems used for worker tracking or performance monitoring. General employment protections slow workforce restructuring, though this occupation group faces minimal high-risk AI Act classifications.
children.uk_rationale: Minimal regulatory friction for refuse workers as AI deployment is primarily in route optimization and scheduling systems rather than worker-affecting decisions. UK's lighter regulatory approach allows slightly easier implementation compared to EU, with no sector-specific restrictions on waste management technology.

children.name: Other elementary workers
children.rationale: These elementary service occupations involve primarily physical tasks, manual labor, and real-time human interaction in unstructured environments. While some administrative aspects (like ticket checking or basic customer service) could be automated, the core work requires physical presence, manual dexterity, and situational awareness that AI cannot easily replicate.
children.regulatory_friction: GDPR Article 22 may limit automated decision-making for customer service interactions, and general employment protection laws slow workforce restructuring. However, since most tasks don't trigger AI Act high-risk classifications, regulatory friction is minimal.
key_protected_tasks: real-time security monitoring and emergency response
children.uk_rationale: These elementary service roles have minimal AI exposure, with any automated systems (scheduling, task allocation) facing negligible UK regulatory friction. The Equality Act could theoretically apply to AI-assisted scheduling or performance monitoring, but practical risk is very low given the limited AI deployment in these manual service roles.
